_meta.note: Human expert answers = the reward signal (Assumption 4: human answers are ground truth). gold_evidence is the deterministic key for evidence-coverage scoring. expected_v1_weakness documents what a no-memory AI typically misses — useful for demoing the gap.

answers.answer: The v4.2.0 release was delayed because its production deploy on June 3 had to be rolled back. The order-tagging migration (commit a3f9c2, from feature NW-1042) created an index on the orders table using a plain CREATE INDEX instead of CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY. On a table that large this took an exclusive lock for ~6 minutes, which blocked all checkout writes and spiked checkout errors to ~40%. This violated our Database Migration Guidelines, which require CONCURRENTLY on large tables like orders. We rolled back, recreated the index concurrently (commit b7e1d4), and re-released as v4.2.1 on June 6.
answers.root_cause: A non-concurrent CREATE INDEX migration on the large orders table took an exclusive lock during the deploy, blocking checkout writes — a violation of the migration guidelines.
reasoning_pattern: Start from the symptom: checkout errors / rollback (SLK-001, NW-1098).
Trace to the triggering code change: which migration/commit ran during the deploy (a3f9c2 via NW-1042).
Check whether it violated a documented guideline (WIKI-migrations: CONCURRENTLY rule).
Confirm the fix and the re-release (b7e1d4 -> v4.2.1).
answers.expected_v1_weakness: A no-memory AI tends to read only the Slack incident thread and report the SYMPTOM ('checkout errors spiked, deploy rolled back') without identifying the non-concurrent migration as the root cause, without citing commit a3f9c2, and without noting the violated migration guideline.

answers.answer: On June 10 payments-gateway p99 latency jumped from ~250ms to ~8s with no error spike. The cause was commit c4d8e1 (feature NW-1120), which added a synchronous, un-timed call to the fraud-check service inside the payment authorization hot path. When fraud-check got slow under afternoon peak load, every authorization blocked on it. This violated the Service Call Guidelines, which require hot-path calls to non-critical services to be async or have a <200ms timeout plus a fallback. We fixed it in commit d9a2f7 (NW-1135) by adding a 150ms timeout and a fail-open fallback.
answers.root_cause: A new synchronous, un-timed fraud-check call (commit c4d8e1) was added to the payment hot path; when that dependency slowed under load it blocked all authorizations — a violation of the hot-path service-call guideline.
reasoning_pattern: Start from the symptom: p99 latency spike (SLK-010, NW-1130).
Trace to the triggering code change: the new fraud-check call (c4d8e1 via NW-1120).
Check whether it violated a documented guideline (WIKI-service-calls: hot-path rule).
answers.expected_v1_weakness: Without the lesson from Q-001, a first answer often blames 'fraud-check is slow' (a symptom of the dependency) rather than identifying the real root cause: that a synchronous un-timed call was put on the hot path against the guidelines. This is the SAME reasoning pattern as Q-001, so a system that learned from Q-001 should get Q-002 right on the first try.

answers.answer: inventory-svc is owned by Sam Okoro on the Catalog team. For a stuck inventory sync, contact Sam — stuck syncs are almost always consumer lag on the stock-updates Kafka topic.
reasoning_pattern: Look up the service in the service catalog for owner + team.
Corroborate with the historical Slack Q&A about inventory sync.
answers.expected_v1_weakness: Usually answered well even at v1; mainly tests evidence coverage across the catalog and the Slack thread.

answers.answer: We chose Postgres over DynamoDB (ADR-007) for three reasons: (1) we need real multi-item ACID transactions (an order writes an orders row + N order_items + a payments link atomically), and DynamoDB's transaction support was too limited for that pattern; (2) finance/analytics run relational joins natively in Postgres; (3) both teams already operate Postgres. Our peak write volume fits a tuned Postgres primary with read replicas.
reasoning_pattern: Find the relevant ADR for the decision (ADR-007).
Summarize the rationale and the rejected alternative.
Corroborate with the architecture Slack discussion.
answers.expected_v1_weakness: Single-source ADR question; v1 is usually strong. Good control case to show the system doesn't over-trigger learning when it's already correct.

answers.answer: v4.3.0 is blocked solely by NW-1148 — flaky integration tests on checkout-svc caused by a race condition in the test harness (order created before the inventory mock is ready). Per the release process, we won't tag a release candidate until CI is green. There are no other open blockers on the v4.3.0 epic (NW-1150).
answers.root_cause: Release blocked by a single P1: flaky checkout-svc integration tests (NW-1148), a test-harness race condition.
reasoning_pattern: Find the release epic and read its 'blocked_by' field (NW-1150 -> NW-1148).
Read the blocker to explain WHY (test-harness race).
Apply the release-process rule (no RC until CI green).
answers.expected_v1_weakness: A no-memory AI may list the blocker ticket id without explaining the underlying cause (test-harness race) or connecting the release-process rule. Benefits from the same 'trace to the underlying cause + check the guideline' pattern.
